title: Weekly synthesis: IPv4, CIDR, Subnet Mask
objective: Lock in the week's material, surface weak spots, and tidy your notes.
week_focus: IPv4 addressing, subnetting, and address planning
checkpoint: Can read and plan IPv4 subnets for small environments.
resource_names: Cisco CCST Networking / Network Technician path
plain_language_intro: Today reviews the week's main concepts as one connected model.
mini_lecture_markdown:
This is the review day for IPv4 addressing, subnetting, and address planning. The goal is to connect the week's main ideas into one working model rather than introduce a new topic. The core terms are IPv4, CIDR, Subnet Mask, Network ID, Host Range, and Broadcast Address.

Across the week, the key mechanisms were: IPv4: uses 32-bit addresses and header fields so routers can forward packets between networks CIDR: expresses the network prefix length so devices can derive which address bits identify the network and which identify hosts Subnet Mask: marks which address bits are network bits so hosts can decide whether traffic is local or remote Network ID: applies the subnet mask to an address, keeps the network bits, and treats the result as the subnet identifier The value of the review is in seeing how these pieces connect across setup, operation, failure, and troubleshooting.

For CompTIA Network+ (N10-009), this block is most useful when you can separate similar concepts, place them at the right layer, and connect them to symptoms or evidence. That usually means following a chain from cause to behavior to visible result.

At this point, the week should read as one sequence rather than separate definitions. Each concept should connect to a role, a mechanism, and a likely scenario where it matters.

One useful review lens is to track how the week's concepts relate to symptoms, evidence, and next-step decisions in a short scenario.

The main point of the review is to keep the concepts connected rather than treat them as isolated terms.
exam_relevance_markdown: Expect this material to show up as scenario-style reasoning rather than raw recall. Typical tasks include distinguishing related concepts, choosing the right component or control, identifying the most likely cause from symptoms, and explaining why one workflow step belongs before another. If you can reconstruct the week without the notes open, you are closer to the level these exams reward.
key_distinctions: IPv4 vs IP Address — IPv4: 32-bit addressing protocol widely used for internet and private networks. IP Address: Logical network address that identifies a source or destination host for routed traffic.
CIDR vs Subnet Mask — CIDR: Classless Inter-Domain Routing notation for subnet masks and prefixes. Subnet Mask: Bitmask that separates network and host portions of an IP address.
Subnet Mask vs CIDR — Subnet Mask: Bitmask that separates network and host portions of an IP address. CIDR: Classless Inter-Domain Routing notation for subnet masks and prefixes.

Network ID vs Host Range — Network ID: The network portion of an IPv4 subnet that identifies the subnet itself rather than an individual host. Host Range: Set of usable host addresses inside a subnet, excluding reserved network and broadcast values.

Host Range vs Network ID — Host Range: Set of usable host addresses inside a subnet, excluding reserved network and broadcast values. Network ID: The network portion of an IPv4 subnet that identifies the subnet itself rather than an individual host.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. IPv4: uses 32-bit addresses and header fields so routers can forward packets between networks CIDR: expresses the network prefix length so devices can derive which address bits identify the network and which identify hosts Subnet Mask: marks which address bits are network bits so hosts can decide whether traffic is local or remote Network ID: applies the subnet mask to an address, keeps the network bits, and treats the result as the subnet identifier That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
self_check_prompts: Explain the difference between IPv4 and CIDR without using the words "better" or "faster" as your whole answer.
Describe what IPv4 does, what input it works on, and what evidence would show that it is functioning correctly.
Walk through today's objective in sequence and say which step would fail first if the system were misconfigured.

Rebuild the week from memory in under three minutes and note which transition between concepts is still weak.
quick_recap: This review day consolidates IPv4, CIDR, and Subnet Mask into one connected model.
